skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render React and Remotion component code into MP4 videos through the inference.sh CLI.
skill.summary: Create MP4 videos from Remotion TSX components with configurable render settings.
skill.author: skillssh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backtick patterns, but these are Markdown inline code and fenced examples rather than Ruby shell execution. The real risk is that the skill authorizes Bash access to the networked infsh CLI and sends user-provided TSX code to a remote Remotion renderer.

medium_findings.title: Networked CLI Renders User-Supplied Code
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to submit React and Remotion TSX code to inference.sh for rendering. This appears intentional and useful, but users should treat submitted code and props as data shared with a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The description and allowed-tools declaration show intentional use of the infsh CLI, and the quick-start sends TSX code as input. This is a real data exposure concern, but there is no evidence of malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Broad infsh Command Allowance
medium_findings.description: The allowed-tools setting permits Bash execution for any infsh command. This is necessary for the documented workflow, but it expands the action surface beyond a single fixed render command.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tool allowance is explicit and semantically relevant. Risk depends on the runtime permissions of infsh and the user's account, so this is a moderate concern rather than confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across many lines. Review found Markdown inline code and fenced bash, TSX, and Python examples, not Ruby backtick execution in executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited ranges are documentation examples and Markdown formatting. No Ruby source file or executable Ruby backtick expression was found in the only scanned file.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Service References
low_findings.description: The detected URLs point to inference.sh, a hosted image, and documentation pages for Remotion and inference.sh. These references are expected for a skill that uses a remote render service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible user-facing links and match the declared service. They do not show hidden callbacks, credential submission, or suspicious domains.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Not Supported by Context
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm indicators, but the cited lines describe video rendering metadata and an input table. No cryptographic function or security-sensitive hashing use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is descriptive Markdown, not code. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic logic in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill authorizes Bash execution of infsh commands and provides examples that run infsh app commands. This is expected behavior but should be visible to users before installation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Bash allowance and CLI examples are explicit. The pattern is dangerous in general because it executes local commands, although the command family is constrained to infsh.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Dependency
dangerous_patterns.description: The render flow depends on inference.sh and related hosted endpoints. Video code, props, and render metadata may leave the local environment during normal use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network service is part of the declared workflow and documentation. The exact data handling behavior is not described in the skill, so the risk is clear but bounded.

content.value_statement: Teams need a repeatable way to turn React animations into finished video files. This skill uses the inference.sh Remotion renderer to create MP4 output from TSX components.
actual_capabilities: Runs inference.sh Remotion render jobs from TSX component input.
Supports common Remotion imports such as AbsoluteFill, Sequence, spring, and interpolate.
Accepts render settings for width, height, frames per second, duration, and codec.
Passes props into the rendered component for reusable video templates.
Shows CLI and Python SDK workflows for rendering and streaming progress.
limitations: Requires an installed and authenticated inference.sh CLI.
Sends render code and inputs to a remote inference.sh service.
Only documents React and Remotion component rendering, not full video editing.
Render availability, cost, and output limits depend on the external service.

use_cases.title: Generate Marketing Motion Clips
use_cases.description: Create short branded MP4 animations from reusable Remotion components.

use_cases.title: Automate Data-Driven Video
use_cases.description: Render charts, counters, and status visuals from component props.

use_cases.title: Prototype React Animation Output
use_cases.description: Test whether a Remotion component renders correctly as a video file.

prompt_templates.title: Render a Simple Intro
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Remotion component for a three second title intro and render it as a 1080p MP4.

prompt_templates.title: Render with Custom Settings
prompt_templates.prompt: Render this Remotion TSX component at 1080 by 1080, 60 FPS, with a five second duration.

prompt_templates.title: Render a Reusable Template
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Remotion template that accepts title and subtitle props, then render one video using these values.

prompt_templates.title: Render and Track Progress
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Python SDK pattern to run a Remotion render and report progress until the final video URL is available.

output_examples.input: A request to render a fade-in welcome title for three seconds.
output_examples.output: A completed MP4 render link with the requested duration, resolution, and frame rate.

output_examples.input: A square animated counter component with a five second duration.
output_examples.output: A 1080 by 1080 MP4 animation showing the counter progression.

output_examples.input: A template render with title and subtitle props.
output_examples.output: A personalized motion graphic video using the supplied text values.

best_practices: Review TSX code before sending it to the remote renderer.
Keep props small and avoid including secrets or private data.
Specify duration, frame rate, and resolution for predictable output.
anti_patterns: Do not submit proprietary source code without approval.
Do not include API keys, credentials, or private customer data in props.
Do not assume the renderer replaces manual review of final video quality.

faq.question: What does this skill render?
faq.answer: It renders React and Remotion TSX components into MP4 video output through inference.sh.

faq.question: Does it need a local Remotion project?
faq.answer: The documented workflow sends component code to the inference.sh render app, not a local Remotion build.

faq.question: Can it pass data into a component?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show props being passed into the rendered component.

faq.question: Is the render local?
faq.answer: No. The skill uses the inference.sh CLI and depends on a remote service.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should users avoid sending?
faq.answer: Users should avoid sending secrets, private customer data, or proprietary code without approval.
